_meta.description: Geographic places used by SoundAtlas events.

places.name: South Bronx
places.summary: Regionaler Kontext fuer viele fruehe Hip-Hop-Partys, Crews und soziale Bedingungen.

places.name: New York City
places.summary: Stadtweiter Kontext fuer Blackout, Medien, Plattenindustrie und Club-Verbreitung.

places.name: 1520 Sedgwick Avenue
places.summary: Apartmenthaus, dessen Gemeinschaftsraum als symbolischer Knotenpunkt der fruehen Hip-Hop-Geschichte gilt.

places.name: Cedar Park
places.summary: Oeffentlicher Raum im West Bronx-Kontext, verbunden mit frueher DJ- und Park-Party-Kultur.

places.name: Bronx River Houses
places.summary: Wohnkomplex im Soundview-Kontext, eng verbunden mit Afrika Bambaataa und frueher Szeneorganisation.

places.name: Disco Fever
places.summary: Bronx-Club an Jerome Avenue und 167th Street, wichtig fuer den Uebergang von lokaler Szene zu Club- und Medienpraesenz.

places.name: The Roxy
places.summary: Downtown-Club und Roller-Rink an der West 18th Street, relevant fuer Hip-Hop als Club-, Kunst- und Medienphaenomen.

places.name: Sugar Hill Records / Englewood
places.summary: Externer Industrie-Knoten in Englewood, New Jersey, verbunden mit Sugar Hill Records und Rapper's Delight.

places.name: The Loft
places.summary: David Mancuso's private party space on Broadway, central to early New York disco and sound-system culture.

places.name: The Gallery
places.summary: An early disco club context around Nicky Siano, important for DJ dramaturgy and club infrastructure.

places.name: Studio 54
places.summary: A Midtown club that made disco visible in 1977 as a glamorous, media-driven pop phenomenon.

places.name: Paradise Garage
places.summary: A King Street club closely tied to Larry Levan, queer club culture, sound-system design, and garage sound.

places.name: West End Records
places.summary: A New York label node for disco, boogie, post-disco, 12-inch, and club culture.

places.name: Prelude Records
places.summary: A label context for post-disco, synth-heavy club music, and remix culture in the early 1980s.

places.name: Bay Ridge
places.summary: A Brooklyn context for the pop myth around Saturday Night Fever and everyday disco culture.

places.name: WBLS / NYC Radio
places.summary: A radio and media node linking club response, DJs, labels, and broader pop reception.

places.name: CBGB
places.summary: Bowery music club that became the central venue for New York punk and new wave bands in the 1970s.

places.name: Max's Kansas City
places.summary: Park Avenue South venue that connected Warhol-era art rock, glam, proto-punk, and later punk booking.

places.name: Mercer Arts Center
places.summary: Greenwich Village arts complex associated with early 1970s glam and proto-punk performance before its 1973 collapse.

places.name: Artists Space
places.summary: Downtown art space linked to the 1978 no wave festival that helped shape the No New York compilation.

places.name: Mudd Club
places.summary: TriBeCa club where post-punk, no wave, performance, fashion, and downtown art scenes overlapped.

places.name: Tier 3
places.summary: Downtown venue remembered for experimental performance, no wave, post-punk, and early 1980s scene crossover.

places.name: Danceteria
places.summary: Multi-format nightclub that linked new wave, DJs, video, dance culture, and downtown crossover audiences.

places.name: 99 Records
places.summary: Greenwich Village store and label connected to no wave, post-punk, post-disco, and independent downtown music.

places.name: Palladium Ballroom
places.summary: Former Midtown ballroom remembered as a mambo-era center whose dance culture shaped later New York salsa audiences.

places.name: East Harlem / El Barrio
places.summary: Puerto Rican and broader Latin neighborhood context for boogaloo, Latin soul, salsa audiences, and Nuyorican cultural life.

places.name: Fania Records / NYC
places.summary: New York label and media node that organized, recorded, marketed, and circulated salsa as a global urban sound.

places.name: Cheetah
places.summary: Midtown nightclub where the 1971 Fania All-Stars concert helped make salsa visible as a mass urban phenomenon.

places.name: Yankee Stadium
places.summary: Bronx stadium used as a marker of salsa's move from neighborhood and club scenes to large-scale concert spectacle.

places.name: El Museo del Barrio
places.summary: East Harlem cultural institution founded to center Puerto Rican, Caribbean, Latin American, and Latino art and history.

places.name: Nuyorican Poets Cafe
places.summary: Lower East Side cultural space that gave performance language to Nuyorican identity, poetry, theater, music, and urban experience.

places.name: Casa Amadeo
places.summary: Bronx Latin music store and community memory node representing record retail, local knowledge, and long-running Latin music infrastructure.

places.name: Slugs' Saloon
places.summary: East Village jazz club associated with free jazz, Sun Ra, Albert Ayler, Rashied Ali, and adventurous late-1960s audiences.

places.name: Studio Rivbea
places.summary: Sam and Bea Rivers' Bond Street loft, one of the central artist-run spaces of the 1970s New York loft jazz scene.

places.name: Ali's Alley
places.summary: Rashied Ali's musician-run loft and label context, part of the wider downtown loft jazz infrastructure.

places.name: The Kitchen
places.summary: Experimental art and performance institution founded in 1971 that connected video, music, dance, performance, and new media.

places.name: Experimental Intermedia
places.summary: Loft-based intermedia and experimental music context associated with drone, film, performance, and long-running avant-garde presentations.

places.name: Roulette
places.summary: Artist-run presenter founded in 1978 for experimental music, improvisation, dance, intermedia, and one-time performance projects.
